coredata.dc:title: Integrated flexible photocatalytic composite nanofiber membranes combined in-situ grown CQDs/g-C3N4 with thermally etched porous silica
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Fei
Guo, Shenjia
Yan, Yi
Zhang, Wu
Cao, Jingguo
Li, Guangbi
Ji, Yanyan
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, various semiconductor photocatalysts have been developed for wastewater treatment. However, traditional bulk photocatalysts suffer from powder agglomeration, difficult recovery, and secondary pollution, which hinders their large-scale application. Herein, the in-situ growth of carbon quantum dots/graphitic carbon nitride (CQDs/g-C3N4) was successfully achieved on flexible silica nanofiber membranes (SiO2 NFMs), which were synthesized by combining electrospinning with thermal polycondensation. It was found that the conversion of smooth membranes into porous surfaces due to the thermal etching effect of alkaline atmosphere during the creation of CQDs/g-C3N4, which not only facilitated the adsorption of tetracycline (TC), but also provided more active sites for photodegradation. Under visible light irradiation, the obtained CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs showed exceptional photocatalytic activity toward TC, with first order kinetic constants of 3.88 and 48.86 times greater than g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs and SiO2 NFMs, respectively. The radical trapping experiments revealed that •O2 −, 1O2, h+, and •OH were the active species in photocatalytic degradation. The photocatalytic degradation was evaluated under initial pH, inorganic anions, and natural aqueous substrate, which shows good applicability. In addition, CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs exhibited high stability and reusability. And the integrated composite membranes can be directly recovered from water after the immobilization of photocatalysts. To sum up, with the advantages of high photocatalytic activity, flexibility, easy separation, and recovery, CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs are prospective catalysts for practical applications in water remediation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrospinning
Porous
In-situ
CQDs/g-C3N4
Integrated flexible photocatalytic membranes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs in different aqueous matrices

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Tianjin Municipal Science and Technology Bureau

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns and (b) FTIR spectra of SiO2 NFMs, CQDs/g-C3N4, and CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs.
(a) Digital images of SiO2 NFMs and (b) CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs; (c) SEM images of SiO2 NFMs and (d) CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs; (e-f) EDS elemental mapping of CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs.
N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms for SiO2 NFMs and CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs.
XPS spectra of (a) C 1s, (b) N 1s, (c) O 1s and (d) Si 2p for CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs.

(a) Visible photocatalytic performance of various photocatalysts for TC and their (b) photocatalytic degradation rate constants k; (c) effect of inorganic anions and (d) initial pH values on the photocatalytic degradation of TC on CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs.
(a) Repeated experiments of TC removal by CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs; (b) XRD patterns and (c) FT-IR spectra of CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs before and after repeated use.
Photocatalytic activity of CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs on TC in the presence of various quenchers.
Photocatalytic degradation of CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs in different aqueous matrices.
The synthesis sketchmap of CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs.
Photocatalytic mechanism for the degradation of TC by CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs.

head.ce:title: Integrated flexible photocatalytic composite nanofiber membranes combined in-situ grown CQDs/g-C3N4 with thermally etched porous silica

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, Tiangong University, Tianjin 300387, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology
State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, Tiangong University, Tianjin 300387, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, various semiconductor photocatalysts have been developed for wastewater treatment. However, traditional bulk photocatalysts suffer from powder agglomeration, difficult recovery, and secondary pollution, which hinders their large-scale application. Herein, the in-situ growth of carbon quantum dots/graphitic carbon nitride (CQDs/g-C3N4) was successfully achieved on flexible silica nanofiber membranes (SiO2 NFMs), which were synthesized by combining electrospinning with thermal polycondensation. It was found that the conversion of smooth membranes into porous surfaces due to the thermal etching effect of alkaline atmosphere during the creation of CQDs/g-C3N4, which not only facilitated the adsorption of tetracycline (TC), but also provided more active sites for photodegradation. Under visible light irradiation, the obtained CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs showed exceptional photocatalytic activity toward TC, with first order kinetic constants of 3.88 and 48.86 times greater than g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs and SiO2 NFMs, respectively. The radical trapping experiments revealed that •O2 −, 1O2, h+, and •OH were the active species in photocatalytic degradation. The photocatalytic degradation was evaluated under initial pH, inorganic anions, and natural aqueous substrate, which shows good applicability. In addition, CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs exhibited high stability and reusability. And the integrated composite membranes can be directly recovered from water after the immobilization of photocatalysts. To sum up, with the advantages of high photocatalytic activity, flexibility, easy separation, and recovery, CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs are prospective catalysts for practical applications in water remediation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracyclines (TC) are broad-spectrum antibacterial drugs, which extensively applied in human medicine and animal husbandry [1]. However, they are not fully absorbed or converted after utilization. Up to 50 % of antibiotics enter the environment throughout their life cycle, which brings out seriously impacts on the ecosystem [2,3]. In addition, antibiotic residues and their metabolites cause antibiotic resistance, chronic toxicity, and endocrine disruption problems [4,5]. Therefore, removing antibiotics from wastewater is crucial for the ecological environment and human health.

As a sustainable, economical, and environmentally friendly technology, photocatalysis is an ideal strategy for removing organic pollutants [6]. Among various photocatalysts, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has become a hot research topic due to its non-toxicity, easy synthesis, stable physicochemical properties, and visible light responsiveness [7,8]. However, g-C3N4 usually exhibits not high photocatalytic activity due to small specific surface area, the easy recombination of photogenerated carriers, and low quantum efficiency [9–11]. Currently, carbon material modification is one of the most popular methods to resolve above problems. And carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with low cost, excellent electron transfer ability, and up-conversion photoluminescence properties are the first choice for carbon modification [12,13].

Additionally, traditional bulk photocatalysts suffer from powder agglomeration, difficult recovery, and secondary pollution, which still need to be addressed in practical applications [14]. To solve above problems, loading photocatalysts onto suitable supports to prepare integrated membranes is an option. Kharroubi et al. prepared Cu-doped ZnO films by ultrasonic spray pyrolysis [15]. Xu et al. synthesized the Z scheme CdS/WO3 on carbon cloth for photocatalytic hydrogen production [16]. Among many supports, nanofiber membranes are of great interest because of their good controllability and high specific surface area. Electrospinning is currently recognized as one of the most promising technologies for fabricating nanofibers (NFs) because of easily operation, economic efficiency, and especially the ability to assemble fibers into materials with specific functions [17,18].

In recent years, electrospinning in conjunction with thermal decomposition has been widely employed to create various composite photocatalysts. Following this way, there are two routes to load photocatalysts onto nanofiber membranes. One route is doping the catalysts into the precursor solution before electrospinning, but the coating into the membranes will lose the photocatalytic center and thus reduce the photocatalytic activity [19–21]. The other route is electrospinning followed by calcination to obtain the photocatalyst on the surface of nanofibers [22]. However, most electrospinning nanofibers are not resistant to high temperatures. The organic fiber will be eliminated during calcination, and the mechanical properties deteriorate [23]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a flexible inorganic nanofiber-based support to address the needs of high performance photocatalysts.

The silicon dioxide nanofiber membranes (SiO2 NFMs) produced by electrospinning are ideal substrates for semiconductor photocatalysts because of facile preparation, great flexibility, superior recyclability, and high thermal stability [24,25]. In addition, SiO2 NFMs have good flexibility and would be suitable for treating continuous wastewater [26]. As far as we know, CQDs or g-C3N4 co-doped SiO2 nanofibers have not been reported.

We prepared a novel flexible CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs with porous structures using electrospinning to combine with a thermal polymerization method. In this study, SiO2 NFMs was chosen as a support substance for the in-situ grown of CQDs/g-C3N4, which could expose their active interfaces. Meanwhile, the SiO2 NFs surface can be etched into porous structure by NH3 generated during the thermal condensation, which is beneficial to the adsorption of pollutants. In addition, SiO2 NFMs were resistant to high temperatures and retains its toughness after calcination. In this research, the photocatalytic performance of CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs were evaluated under visible light irradiation. The phase structure and morphology were determined by various characterization methods. Furthermore, the photocatalytic performance of the composites was studied with changing the initial pH, inorganic anions and aqueous matrices. The powder catalysts were difficult to separate, easy to aggregation and secondary contamination, which could be surmounted by introducing flexible SiO2 NFMs. What's more, there was no discernible decline in photocatalytic performance after repeated use.

All chemicals were analytically pure for direct used. The CQDs were synthesized according to our previous work [27]. Specifically, 80 mL of deionized water (DI) and 4 g of waste pine needle powder were combined and transferred to a 100 mL autoclave before being held at 220 °C for 10 h. The carbon precursor solution containing CQDs was obtained after extraction through a microfiltration membrane with 0.8 μm.

1.5 g polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) powder was dissolved in anhydrous ethanol and stirred continuously for 1 h to prepare a PVP solution (15 wt %), noted as solution A. Under vigorous stirring at room temperature, 0.225 mL deionized water and 15 μL HNO3 (13 mol/L) were added sequentially to 2.6 g tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) solution and were stirred for 1 h, noted as solution B. Then A and B were mixed and agitated for 1 h to form a homogeneous precursor solution. The SiO2 NFMs was fabricated by electrospinning. Briefly, the above mixed solution was placed into a 10 mL syringe with a distance of 20 cm between the pinhead and the collector. A voltage of 10 kV was used and the feed rate of the syringe was 0.5 mm/min. The gathered nanofiber membranes were heated at 800 °C for 2 h at a rate of 5 °C/min. The PVP was removed and SiO2 NFMs were attained during the high-temperature process.

Scheme 1 showed the synthetic route of SiO2 NFMs and CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs obtained by electrostatic spinning combined with thermal polymerization. CQDs solution was added into a urea-containing crucible, stirred to dissolve, and dried at 50 °C. Subsequently, SiO2 NFMs (3 × 3 cm2) was placed on the mixture. The mixture of SiO2 NFMs with CQDs and urea was calcined at 550 °C for 4 h (5 °C/min) to achieve the in-situ growth of CQDs/g-C3N4 on the surface of SiO2 NFMs. The CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs were washed several times with DI and dried at 50 °C after being cooled to room temperature. As a control group, simple SiO2 NFMs were prepared using the above steps.

CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs (3 × 3 cm2) were placed in TC solution (3 mg/L, 50 mL) for 30 min to achieve adsorption equilibrium in the dark. The solution was then irradiated under a Xe lamp. 3 mL sample was obtained every 30 min and filtered through a filter membrane (0.22 μm). The concentration of TC was measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer set at 357 nm.

The phase structures of SiO2 NFMs, CQDs/g-C3N4, and CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs were investigated by XRD. Fig. 1 a showed two diffraction peaks of CQDs/g-C3N4 at 13.2° and 27.1°, which could be indexed as the (100) and (002) crystal planes of g-C3N4, respectively (JCPDS 87-1526) [28,29]. The weak diffraction peaks at 2θ = 22.7° of SiO2 NFMs and CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs corresponded to the (101) crystal plane of amorphous SiO2 [30]. The characteristic diffraction peaks of both SiO2 NFMs and CQDs/g-C3N4 could be observed in the XRD patterns of the CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs composites, indicating the successful in-situ growth of CQDs/g-C3N4 on SiO2 NFs. The structures and functional groups of all samples were characterized further by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 1b). For SiO2 NFMs, the peaks at 1046 cm−1, 803 cm−1, and 460 cm−1 corresponded to SiOSi bonds [31]. For CQDs/g-C3N4, the characteristic peak at 803 cm−1 corresponded to the feature contraction vibration of the s-triazine unit typical of g-C3N4 [32]. The bands from 1000 to 1700 cm−1 were attributed to the CN heterocyclic stretching vibration [33], where the peak at 1234 cm−1 corresponded to the oscillations of the COC group [34]. The bands ranging from 3000 to 3600 cm−1 were consistent with the stretching vibrations of NH or OH [35]. Notably, all characteristic peaks of CQDs/g-C3N4 and SiO2 NFMs appeared in the spectra of CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs composites, indicating the successful in-situ growth of CQDs/g-C3N4 on SiO2 NFs.

As shown in Fig. 2 a–b, SiO2 NFMs and CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs obtained by electrostatic spinning and thermal polycondensation were mechanically flexible. The microstructure and morphology of SiO2 NFMs and CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs were investigated by SEM. Fig. 2c showed that the surface of SiO2 NFs was smooth and uniform and the average diameter of fibers were around 520 nm. It could be found in Fig. 2d that some attachments could be observed on the surface of SiO2 NFs, indicating the successful in-situ growth of CQDs/g-C3N4 on SiO2 NFs without serious aggregation. After introducing CQDs/g-C3N4, most of the nanofibers were fractured and porous due to the alkaline thermal etching of SiO2 by ammonia generated from urea during the calcination process. Compared with the bulk samples, the CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs provided a higher specific surface area and abundant active sites [36]. EDS results showed that C, N, O, and Si were distributed equally throughout the membranes (Fig. 2e). Meanwhile, in Fig. 2f, no characteristic peaks of other elements were detected out, save for C, N, O, and Si, further indicating that CQDs/g-C3N4 were grown in-situ on SiO2 NFs.

The main elements and chemical states of CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs were investigated by high-resolution XPS. The full spectra (Fig. 4 a) showed four major peaks of C, N, O, and Si, indicating the successful in-situ grown of CQDs/g-C3N4. To further determined the valence state of each element of membranes, high-resolution XPS were performed on C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and Si 2p (Fig. 4 a–d). The peak at 288.08 eV in the C 1s (Fig. 4a) was attributed to the C atom in NCN, with the structural features of g-C3N4 [37]. The peaks at 286.34 and 284.80 eV related to hydroxyl carbon (COH) and surface indeterminate hydrocarbons, respectively [38,39]. The peaks at 404.42 eV, 400.30 eV, and 398.52 eV in the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 4b) corresponded to the N atom of the graphitic aromatic triazine ring, the amino group (C-NH) and the sp2 hybridized nitrogen (CNC), respectively [40–42]. The O 1s spectrum had three convolution peaks are present (Fig. 4c). The peaks at 532.48 eV and 531.40 eV represent COC and COH, respectively [43,44]. The peak at 533.58 eV was identified as lattice oxygen in the SiO2 structure (SiOSi) [45]. The lower peak intensity might be due to that the surface of SiO2 NFs was covered by the in-situ growth of CQDs/g-C3N4. In Fig. 4d, the Si 2p peak at 103.08 eV was associated with SiOSi in SiO2 and reflected the typical structure of SiO2 [46]. In summary, for the CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs composites, all typical peaks of Si, O, C, and N atoms could be observed, confirming the successful in-situ growth of CQDs/g-C3N4 on SiO2 NFs.

TC was chosen as the target pollutant to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of catalysts and nanofiber membranes (Fig. 5 a). Without photocatalyst, almost no degradation of TC was observed, indicating that TC was weakly self-degraded under natural conditions. Under visible light irradiation, TC was almost not photodegraded by SiO2 NFMs, indicating that the photocatalytic ability of SiO2 NFMs was negligible. The introduction of g-C3N4 and CQDs/g-C3N4 on SiO2 NFMs resulted in a significant enhancement of TC adsorption, which was due to the porous structure of SiO2 NFMs caused by alkaline thermal etching during calcination. The reaction rate constant of CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs was 0.01710 min−1, which was 48.86 and 3.88 times higher than that of SiO2 NFMs (0.00035 min−1) and g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs (0.00497 min−1), respectively (Fig. 5b). The loading of CQDs/g-C3N4 onto SiO2 NFMs with large specific surface areas can endow the photocatalyst with more active sites and inhibit unnecessary aggregation of the catalyst particles. Meanwhile, the porous structure of SiO2 NFMs were rich in mesopores, which could promote mass transfer in the surface reaction [47]. In addition, the excellent up-conversion fluorescence properties of CQDs enhanced the light absorption and promoted the generation of additional photoinduced carriers [48].

Several inorganic anions such as Cl, SO4 2−, NO3 − and H2PO4 − often coexisted in the wastewater, so NaCl, Na2SO4, NaHCO3, KNO3, and NaH2PO4 were introduced to investigate their effects on TC removal. The addition of Cl, SO4 2− and NO3 − had almost no impact compared to the control experiment (Fig. 5c). However, the presence of HCO3 − inhibited the degradation, and the removal rate was only about 73.82 % in 150 min. It was because that HCO3 − was a scavenger of RSs during photocatalytic oxidation [49]. Furthermore, it was observed that H2PO4 − also significantly inhibited the degradation of TC, and the corresponding removal efficiency decreased to 69.77 %. The decrease was related to the reaction or depletion of reactive substances such as •OH and h+ (Eqs. (1)–(2)), which was because H2PO4 − was a weaker oxidant than •OH, thus affecting the photocatalytic activity [50].
(1) H2PO4 − + •OH → H2PO4• + OH−
(2) H2PO4 − + h+ → H2PO4•

The initial pH of the solution was one of the environmental factors that affect photocatalysis. We investigated the removal rate of TC at various pH values and the best catalytic activity was achieved at pH = 6.7. In Fig. 5d, the removal efficiency increased from 73.73 % to 90.03 % as the initial pH increased from 3 to 6.7, and the removal efficiency decreased by 16.32 % when the pH was further increased to 11. Based on these results, the removal efficiency decreased slightly when the TC solution was subjected to strong acids and bases, which could be attributed to the consumption of •OH by H+ in the presence of strong acids (Eq. (3)) [51]. Under alkaline conditions, both TCs and CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs were electronegative and repelled each other, which led to poor dark adsorption effects and photocatalytic activity [52]. In a word, CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs could effectively remove TC in the pH range of 3 to 11, namely which had a relatively wide pH applicability range.
(3) •OH + H+ + e− → H2O

In Fig. 6 a, the photocatalytic efficiency of CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs for TC removal did not decrease significantly after five consecutive cycles, and the corresponding photodegradation efficiency remained at 83.74 %. Meanwhile, to further investigate the photocatalytic stability, the XRD patterns and FT-IR spectra of CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs were examined before and after the reaction. The results showed that their phase structures and chemical groups were almost unchanged after five cycles (Fig. 6b–c). The in-situ reaction of CQDs/g-C3N4 on SiO2 NFs might lead to chemical bonding in the micro-regional heterojunctions of CQDs/g-C3N4. Meanwhile, the porous structure could minimize agglomeration during the photocatalytic reaction. Therefore, effective charge separation could be maintained after repeated use. In addition, the flexible CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs with porous structures could be easily separated from solution. In summary, CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs were reusable and stable in the photocatalytic process.

The reactive species in the photodegradation of TC by CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs were investigated by trapping experiments. Ascorbic acid (AA), furfuryl alcohol (FAH), Na2C2O4, and isopropyl alcohol (IPA) were added as scavengers of superoxide radicals (•O2 −), singlet oxygen (1O2), vacancies (h+), and hydroxyl radicals (•OH). In Fig. 7 , the presence of IPA and Na2C2O4 slightly inhibited the photocatalytic activity, with a decrease in removal rate of 9.61 % and 14.59 %, respectively. In contrast, in the presence of AA and FAH, the degradation was significantly inhibited, with the removal rates decreasing to 25.12 % and 52.01 %, respectively, indicating that •O2 − and 1O2 were the primary radicals for the photodegradation. Notably, the standard redox potentials of OH−/•OH or H2O/•OH were more positive than those of CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs. Therefore, it was difficult for CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs to oxidize OH− or H2O to •OH, which might originate from •O2 − (Eq. (4)–(5)) [53]. In summary, •O2 −, 1O2, h+, and •OH were active species in the photodegradation, among which •O2 − and 1O2 played crucial roles, and the effects of h+ and •OH were negligible.
(4) •O2 − + e− + 2H+ → H2O2
(5) H2O2 + e− → OH− + •OH

We proposed a possible mechanism of photocatalysis for the degradation of TC by CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs based on the above results (Scheme 2 ). Due to the porous structure of CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs, the TC in solution was adequate with the fiber membrane and adsorbed on it. Under visible light irradiation, the electrons in the valence band (VB) of CQDs/g-C3N4 transited to the conduction band (CB) and generated h+. The large specific surface area of CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs conferred more active sites, facilitating the mass transfer in the surface reaction and reducing the complexation of electron-hole pair. Meanwhile, CQDs with superior up-conversion fluorescence characteristics enhanced light absorption and promoted the generation of more photogenerated carriers. The generated e− transferred to the membrane surface and combined with O2 to form •O2 −, which reacted with h+ to form 1O2. Since the CB potential of CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs was more negative than O2/•O2 −, •O2 − reacted with H+. Eventually, TC that adsorbed on the fiber membrane surface was decomposed and mineralized into H2O and CO2 by the RSs of •O2 −, 1O2, h+, and •OH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs in different aqueous matrices
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate whether CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs could be applied under natural environments, the degradation of TC was carried out in various water matrices. In Fig. 8 , TC removal was reduced in tap water (86.95 %), lake water (77.89 %), and seawater (65.45 %) compared to deionized water (93.02 %). It could be attributed to the ions in water that weaken the required radiation flux of the sample or competed for RSs [46]. Nevertheless, CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs could effectively remove TC from natural water environments.

In conclusion, CQDs/g-C3N4 were successfully in-situ grown on flexible SiO2 NFMs by electrospinning with thermal polycondensation. We found that the ammonia gas generated during the thermal polycondensation etched the SiO2 NFs to give them a porous structure, which facilitated the adsorption of pollutants and dramatically minimizes the agglomeration and loss during the photocatalytic process. The loading of CQDs/g-C3N4 onto the SiO2 NFMs with large specific surface areas endowed the catalyst with more active sites and avoided unnecessary aggregation of the catalyst. Meanwhile, SiO2 NFMs had abundant mesopores, which promoted mass transfer in the surface reaction. In addition, CQDs with superior up-conversion fluorescence characteristics enhanced light absorption and promoted the generation of more photogenerated carriers. As a result, CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs exhibited an excellent TC removal rate of 93.02 %, which was 3.44 and 48.86 times higher than that of SiO2@g-C3N4 NFMs and SiO2 NFMs, respectively. The radical capture experiments revealed that •O2 − and 1O2 were the major active substances for the photocatalytic process. It was found that the in-situ reaction of CQDs/g-C3N4 on SiO2 NFMs may form strong chemical bonds in the microregion heterojunctions of CQDs/g-C3N4, thus, they exhibited high stability and reusability in the photocatalytic process. Most importantly, CQDs/g-C3N4@SiO2 NFMs were flexible and could be recovered from water by direct fishing in practical applications, and it may overcome the problems of aggregation and susceptibility to secondary pollution in photocatalytic reactions and is prospective to be a promising photocatalyst in water remediation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2023.124672.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of tetracycline for tetracycline adjuvant screening in bacteria
Sensor Actuat. B-Chem.

Front. Environ. Sci.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of antibiotics consumption on the behavior of airborne antibiotic resistance genes in chicken farms
J. Hazard. Mater.
